source_title: Estimating electricity saving-potential in small offices using adaptive thermal comfort
article_summary: The paper quantifies electricity-saving potential in small office buildings using smart thermostat control, adaptive thermal comfort, occupancy-informed HVAC and smart plug control, and night-purge free cooling. It argues that small offices often lack building management systems and therefore miss energy-saving opportunities such as adaptive cooling setpoints, occupancy-based operation, plug-load shutdown, and natural ventilation. Simulations across major U.S. climate zones show that combined measures can reduce total electricity consumption by 8.9% to 20.4%, equivalent to 12.2 to 30.4 kWh/m2 per year. The most relevant rule-generation ideas are adaptive cooling setpoints based on outdoor temperature history, occupancy-aware HVAC and plug-load control, smart plug shutdown of non-critical appliances, and weather-aware night purge ventilation when outdoor air is suitable.

chunks.chunk_title: Small offices lack smart control despite large energy-saving potential
chunks.text: Small office buildings below 10,000 square feet account for approximately 75% of office buildings in the United States. They often use packaged HVAC systems and usually lack building management systems. This absence of smart control leaves missed energy-efficiency opportunities, including fixed cooling setpoint schedules that ignore comfort needs and real occupancy patterns, and equipment or appliance energy waste when devices are not being used.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to justify transforming static time-based HVAC and appliance rules into sensor-informed rules that reduce waste while preserving comfort and intended service.

chunks.chunk_title: IoT sensors and remotely controlled devices enable energy-aware smart control
chunks.text: Smart control informed by IoT sensors and enabled by remotely controlled devices can optimize building operation to minimize unnecessary energy consumption and improve indoor thermal comfort. The paper evaluates adaptive setpoint temperature, occupancy-based HVAC control, occupancy-informed plug-load control, and night-purge free cooling as deployable smart control measures.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this as a general principle for adding sensor-based conditions and controllable-device actions to greener IF-THEN rules.

chunks.chunk_title: Combined smart controls can significantly reduce total electricity use
chunks.text: The simulated combination of adaptive cooling setpoints, occupancy-based HVAC and plug-load control, and night-purge free cooling can reduce total electricity consumption in small offices by 8.9% to 20.4%, equivalent to annual reductions of 12.2 to 30.4 kWh/m2. Hot and dry climates receive the largest percentage savings.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to prioritize combinations of green rule modifications rather than isolated actions when multiple conditions can be safely added.

chunks.chunk_title: Cooling electricity is a major target for energy reduction
chunks.text: Building electricity use is a major energy category, and space cooling is a significant contributor. Reducing cooling energy can reduce total energy consumption, lower grid stress, support more economical operation, and lower carbon intensity.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to prioritize greener transformations of cooling-related rules, especially rules that lower setpoints unnecessarily or run cooling without occupancy need.

chunks.chunk_title: Fixed thermostat schedules can waste energy and reduce comfort
chunks.text: Packaged air-conditioning equipment in small offices often operates using fixed schedules of indoor air temperature setpoints by day and time. These static schedules may fail to reflect actual comfort preferences, occupancy patterns, or weather conditions, leading to unnecessary cooling or insufficient comfort.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to transform fixed thermostat schedules into adaptive schedules that include occupancy, comfort, and outdoor-temperature awareness.

chunks.chunk_title: Overcooling wastes energy and may reduce occupant comfort
chunks.text: Office overcooling is common, especially during hot summer days, and can cause occupants to wear additional clothing indoors. The paper notes that traditional cooling temperature rules are based on outdated assumptions and may not represent all occupants' comfort preferences. Avoiding overcooling can save energy while improving thermal comfort.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to raise overly aggressive cooling setpoints or add comfort-feedback constraints instead of blindly cooling to low temperatures.

chunks.chunk_title: Thermal feedback channels can improve comfort-preserving efficiency
chunks.text: A technical barrier to flexible cooling setpoints is the lack of an effective feedback channel and setpoint-control access. Occupants often cannot express thermal feedback conveniently, and buildings may lack mechanisms to collect feedback and adjust the setpoint accordingly. Smart building solutions can use comfort buttons or user apps to collect thermal preferences and adjust setpoints to improve satisfaction.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to add user-feedback exceptions or comfort-preserving checks before increasing HVAC energy-saving aggressiveness.

chunks.chunk_title: Programmable thermostats only save energy when schedules match reality
chunks.text: Programmable thermostats can adjust temperature by time of day and day of week, such as using a comfort setpoint during business hours and a setback at night or on weekends. However, they do not inherently save energy; savings depend on whether users operate them correctly and whether programmed schedules match real occupancy.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to replace or strengthen purely time-based thermostat rules with occupancy-aware conditions.

chunks.chunk_title: Static business-hour assumptions can conflict with occupancy
chunks.text: Assumptions about business hours may not reflect true occupancy. For example, air conditioning may shut off at 6 p.m. while people are still working, or it may operate on Saturday when the office is unoccupied. These mismatches either reduce comfort or waste energy.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to add occupancy override logic: maintain comfort when occupied outside normal hours and reduce conditioning when unoccupied during scheduled hours.

chunks.chunk_title: Motion-sensor thermostats enable dynamic HVAC control
chunks.text: Thermostats embedded with or coupled to motion sensors can capture real occupancy patterns rather than relying only on programmed user settings. This allows temperature control to become more accurate and dynamic, improving HVAC energy efficiency.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to add motion or occupancy conditions to HVAC trigger-action rules.

chunks.chunk_title: Occupancy-centric control applies beyond HVAC
chunks.text: Occupancy-centric operation can also save energy on plug loads and lighting. Plug loads account for a substantial share of commercial building electricity consumption, while equipment and appliances often rely on manual control and continue consuming energy when unused.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to extend occupancy-aware rule generation from HVAC to lights, smart plugs, and non-critical appliances.

chunks.chunk_title: Phantom or vampire loads can be reduced with smart plugs
chunks.text: Equipment and appliances can consume standby power even when not being used. This standby power is described as phantom load or vampire load. Smart plugs and power strips that allow remote control can save substantial energy when coupled with occupancy signals.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to transform rules so that non-critical smart plugs turn off after vacancy or lack of use.

chunks.chunk_title: Natural ventilation can provide free cooling
chunks.text: Many climates provide opportunities to use natural ventilation as free cooling. Fresh outdoor air at suitable temperature and humidity can improve indoor air quality by flushing stale air while saving cooling and fan energy.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to add outdoor temperature and humidity checks before using mechanical cooling, and to suggest window or ventilation actions when conditions are suitable.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge uses suitable nighttime outdoor air for passive cooling
chunks.text: Night purge is a natural ventilation strategy that leaves windows or louvers open at night when weather is suitable. It precools the building using outdoor air and can reduce daytime cooling demand.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to create rules that open windows or enable ventilation at night only under suitable outdoor conditions.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge is best in climates with large day-night temperature swings
chunks.text: Night purge is most effective in climates with substantial diurnal temperature swings and in buildings with significant thermal mass such as concrete, brick, or phase-change material. It is also effective when summer is mild or when warm days are followed by cool nights.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to condition night-purge rules on climate suitability and day-night outdoor temperature difference.

chunks.chunk_title: Window actuators and IoT sensors expand natural ventilation automation
chunks.text: The paper states that increasing adoption of window actuators, IoT sensors, and integrated intelligent control can unlock more natural ventilation opportunities and reduce the load and carbon footprint of building operations.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to suggest automated window or ventilation actions as alternatives to air conditioning when environmental conditions allow.

chunks.chunk_title: Adaptive thermal comfort uses recent outdoor temperature history
chunks.text: The adaptive thermal comfort model relates the zone operative temperature setpoint to the recent history of outdoor air temperatures. It generates a daily cooling setpoint and applies only in cooling mode. During summertime in hot climates, the adaptive setpoint can be higher than a traditional thermostat setpoint, producing HVAC energy savings.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to generate cooling rules whose setpoint adapts to recent outdoor temperatures instead of remaining fixed.

chunks.chunk_title: Adaptive comfort setpoints have applicability bounds
chunks.text: The ASHRAE adaptive comfort model is applicable when the running average outdoor air temperature for the past 30 days is between 10.0°C and 33.5°C. This boundary matters because adaptive setpoint rules should only be applied when outdoor conditions fall within the comfort model's valid range.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to prevent adaptive setpoint rules from being applied outside the supported outdoor-temperature range.

chunks.chunk_title: ASHRAE adaptive central-line setpoint formula
chunks.text: The paper uses the ASHRAE Standard 55 adaptive comfort central line to generate operative temperature setpoints. The central line is calculated as 0.31 times the prevailing mean outdoor temperature plus 17.8°C, and is bounded between 20.9°C and 28.2°C.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this as a concrete method for computing adaptive cooling setpoints when generating thermostat rules.

chunks.chunk_title: ASHRAE adaptive 90% upper-line formula indicates additional savings potential
chunks.text: The paper also evaluates the ASHRAE 55 90% acceptability upper line, calculated as 0.31 times the prevailing mean outdoor temperature plus 20.3°C and bounded between 23.4°C and 30.6°C. It is used to demonstrate greater potential cooling energy savings, although the paper uses the central-line model for the main quantified savings.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this cautiously as an upper comfort-bound reference when generating more aggressive energy-saving setpoints, while preserving occupant comfort.

chunks.chunk_title: Raising cooling setpoints by a few degrees can save substantial cooling energy
chunks.text: In climate zone 4A, adaptive cooling setpoints based on the ASHRAE central line range from 22.2°C to 25.8°C and achieve about 20% cooling energy savings. The 90% upper-line setpoints range from 22.2°C to 28.3°C and achieve about 38% cooling energy savings. These savings include both compressor and fan energy savings.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to justify small upward adjustments to cooling setpoints when comfort constraints permit.

chunks.chunk_title: Weekend and holiday setback behavior can reduce cooling demand
chunks.text: In the simulated adaptive setpoint schedule, weekends and holidays use the same cooling setpoint as the setback temperature, 29°C. This reflects the use of relaxed cooling during periods when the office is expected to be unoccupied or has reduced occupancy.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to add setback temperatures during weekends, holidays, or verified unoccupied periods, with occupancy override if needed.

chunks.chunk_title: Realistic occupancy is dynamic and sub-hourly
chunks.text: The paper uses realistic office occupancy schedules generated at 10-minute intervals from stochastic occupant behavior models. Unlike static assumptions such as 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., dynamic occupancy accounts for day-to-day and person-to-person variation, especially in the early morning, around noon, and in the evening.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to prefer real-time occupancy sensing over coarse fixed schedules when generating efficient rules.

chunks.chunk_title: Smart plugs can shut down non-critical equipment after extended vacancy
chunks.text: Smart plug control automatically turns off non-critical equipment and appliances, such as monitors, televisions, coffee makers, and water heaters, if occupancy is not detected for an extended period.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to add vacancy-duration conditions to smart plug rules controlling non-critical loads.

chunks.chunk_title: Occupancy-centric control saves HVAC and plug-load energy
chunks.text: In climate zone 4A, occupancy-centric control alone achieves 7.7% total electricity savings in small offices by reducing HVAC consumption by 6.7% and plug loads by 28.2%. Electricity intensity is reduced from 132.4 kWh/m2 to 122.2 kWh/m2.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to rank occupancy-based transformations as high-value candidates for greener smart-home automation.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge can waste fan energy when outdoor temperatures are too high
chunks.text: Night purge reduces HVAC energy when the climate is suitable, but when daily mean outdoor air temperature exceeds about 25°C, night ventilation may not effectively reduce daytime HVAC operating time and may instead waste fan energy at night.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this as a constraint: do not activate night purge or fan-based precooling when outdoor temperature is too high to provide useful cooling.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge has modest total-building savings but can reduce cooling energy
chunks.text: For small offices in climate zone 4A, night purge alone saves 3.0% of HVAC energy consumption during the cooling season and about 0.4% of total annual electricity consumption. The energy intensity reduction is about 0.7 kWh/m2, with cooling savings partly offset by a small fan energy increase.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to treat night purge as a conditional optimization with smaller but useful savings, especially when other conditions are favorable.

chunks.chunk_title: Thermal mass improves night-purge effectiveness
chunks.text: Buildings with exposed concrete slabs or other thermal mass benefit from night purge because cool night air lowers slab temperature. The cooled mass then helps reduce daytime HVAC energy consumption by lowering radiant and operative temperatures.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to favor night-purge rules in buildings or rooms where thermal mass can store coolness overnight.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge can synergize with adaptive comfort
chunks.text: When night purge is applied along with phase-change material and adaptive thermal comfort setpoints, it can create a synergistic effect and unlock more energy savings. Cooled phase-change material can absorb heat during the day and help keep indoor temperature close to the adaptive comfort temperature.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to combine adaptive setpoint and night-purge rules where passive cooling can help maintain comfort.

chunks.chunk_title: Combining adaptive setpoints, occupancy control, and night purge gives highest savings
chunks.text: For climate zone 4A, the combination of adaptive setpoint, occupancy-centric control, and night-purge natural ventilation reduces annual electricity consumption by 12.4%. Individual savings are 7.7% from occupancy-centric control, 4.6% from adaptive setpoints, and 0.4% from night purge.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to generate multi-condition green rules that combine occupancy, adaptive temperature, and environmental opportunities.

chunks.chunk_title: Occupancy behavior savings are consistent across climates
chunks.text: Across climate zones, the dynamic occupancy behavior model saves about 7.4% to 9.5% per year. Unlike adaptive thermal comfort and night purge, occupancy-based savings are relatively consistent across climate conditions.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to prioritize occupancy-aware transformations even when climate-specific data is unavailable.

chunks.chunk_title: Adaptive comfort savings depend strongly on climate
chunks.text: Adaptive comfort savings are strongly influenced by climate. Cooling electricity savings decrease from hot to cold climates because cooling energy becomes a smaller share of total electricity use. Dry climates produce higher savings than humid climates because humid climates require latent cooling energy for moisture removal. Marine climates show lower savings because mild summers reduce cooling's share of total electricity use.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to make adaptive cooling transformations climate-aware and humidity-aware.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge savings are climate-specific and limited
chunks.text: Night purge annual savings are limited, about 0.1% to 1% per year across climates. The most effective climate zone identified is 3C, where days are warm and nights are cool. Dry climates with warm days and cool nights are usually worth considering for this measure.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to apply night-purge rules selectively rather than as a universal strategy.

chunks.chunk_title: Combined measures reduce whole-building and HVAC electricity across climates
chunks.text: Across U.S. climate zones, combined energy efficiency measures save 12.2 to 30.4 kWh/m2 per year at the whole-building level. Most savings come from HVAC end use, about 6.5 to 22.6 kWh/m2. Compared with the baseline, combined measures achieve 9% to 20% total-building electricity savings and 23% to 39% HVAC end-use savings.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to evaluate whether rule transformations target high-impact energy end uses and combine complementary strategies.

chunks.chunk_title: Adaptive cooling avoids overcooling through weather-aware comfort
chunks.text: The conclusion states that adaptive cooling setpoints avoid overcooling in commercial buildings by considering people’s adaptive behavior and clothing adjustment against general weather conditions. In climate 2B, adaptive cooling setpoint alone saves 12% of small-office electricity consumption compared with a static 22.2°C cooling setpoint.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to justify replacing fixed cool setpoints with adaptive comfort-preserving setpoints based on outdoor conditions.

chunks.chunk_title: Occupancy-centric control applies to both HVAC and smart plugs
chunks.text: The conclusion emphasizes that IoT sensors detecting occupancy and motion enable occupancy-centric control for both HVAC systems and smart plugs. Reported savings are around 6.7% for HVAC energy consumption and about 28.2% for plug load, with total electricity savings of approximately 7.7% across all climate zones.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to create rules that coordinate HVAC setbacks and appliance shutdown based on occupancy detection.

chunks.chunk_title: Night purge improves indoor air quality while saving cooling energy
chunks.text: Night purge ventilation uses free cooling and can improve indoor air quality. The paper reports that it can reduce up to 3.0% of HVAC cooling energy consumption in climate 4A and about 5.6% of cooling electricity consumption in marine climates such as 3C and 4C.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to support ventilation-based alternatives to mechanical cooling when outdoor air quality and outdoor temperature/humidity are suitable.

chunks.chunk_title: Plug-and-play smart devices can unlock demand and energy reductions
chunks.text: The paper identifies smart thermostats with occupancy sensors, smart plugs, and window actuators as plug-and-play IoT sensing and control devices that can unlock energy-saving and demand-reduction opportunities in small offices.
chunks.rule_generation_use: Use this to map abstract green transformations to concrete smart-home device types.

patterns.pattern_description: Modify cooling rules that use a fixed setpoint so that the setpoint adapts to recent outdoor temperature history, using an adaptive thermal comfort approach rather than always cooling to the same low temperature.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when an HVAC or air-conditioning rule cools to a static setpoint and outdoor conditions suggest that a higher comfort-preserving setpoint is acceptable.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply outside the valid adaptive comfort temperature range, when the user explicitly requires a fixed low temperature, or when comfort or safety would be compromised.

patterns.pattern_description: If a cooling rule appears to overcool a room, raise the cooling setpoint by a small amount while preserving comfort, because small increases in cooling setpoint can significantly reduce compressor and fan energy.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the original rule cools aggressively, such as maintaining a low fixed cooling setpoint regardless of outdoor weather or occupancy.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when cooling is needed for health, equipment protection, humidity control, or an explicit user comfort requirement.

patterns.pattern_description: Add motion or occupancy detection to HVAC rules so heating or cooling follows actual occupancy instead of only a fixed clock schedule.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when an HVAC rule runs during assumed occupied hours or shuts off at a fixed time without checking whether people are actually present.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply if occupancy sensing is unavailable or unreliable, or if the rule protects temperature-sensitive people, pets, equipment, or safety conditions.

patterns.pattern_description: Preserve comfort by allowing occupancy to override scheduled HVAC setbacks when people are present outside normal hours.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule turns HVAC off or uses a large setback at a fixed time even though occupants may still be present.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when the space must remain in setback for safety, maintenance, or explicit user instruction.

patterns.pattern_description: When no occupancy is detected, change HVAC operation to a relaxed setback temperature instead of maintaining full comfort conditioning.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule maintains comfort setpoints even when the room or home is unoccupied.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when minimum or maximum temperature must be maintained for health, pets, humidity, freezing prevention, or equipment protection.

patterns.pattern_description: Modify smart plug rules so non-critical appliances and equipment turn off after no occupancy is detected for an extended period.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply to non-critical devices such as monitors, televisions, coffee makers, and similar plug loads when a space has been vacant for a defined period.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply to critical devices, safety devices, medical equipment, refrigeration, network equipment required by the user, or appliances that should not be power-cycled.

patterns.pattern_description: Use remotely controllable smart plugs or power strips to cut standby consumption from devices that would otherwise continue consuming phantom or vampire power while unused.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule leaves plug-in equipment powered even when not in active use.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when standby power is required for device readiness, updates, safety, data retention, or explicit user preference.

patterns.pattern_description: Extend occupancy-centric control to lighting by using occupancy or motion signals to avoid lighting empty spaces.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when lights are scheduled or triggered without verifying that the space is occupied.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when lighting is required for security, safety, accessibility, or explicit user preference.

patterns.pattern_description: Use nighttime outdoor air as free cooling by opening windows or louvers, or enabling ventilation, when outdoor temperature and humidity are suitable.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply at night when outdoor air is cool and comfortable enough to reduce next-day cooling demand.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when outdoor air is too hot, too humid, unsafe, polluted, rainy, or when open windows would compromise security or comfort.

patterns.pattern_description: Prevent night purge from running when outdoor temperature is too high to provide useful cooling, because fan operation may waste energy without reducing daytime HVAC operation.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule activates night ventilation or fan-based precooling regardless of outdoor temperature.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when a verified control strategy proves that nighttime ventilation still reduces net energy despite warmer outdoor conditions.

patterns.pattern_description: When outdoor air temperature and humidity are suitable, use natural ventilation or outdoor-air cooling opportunities before relying on mechanical cooling.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a cooling rule activates air conditioning even though outdoor conditions could provide free cooling.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when outdoor air quality, humidity, temperature, security, noise, or occupant preference makes natural ventilation unsuitable.

patterns.pattern_description: Generate compound green rules that combine occupancy awareness, adaptive setpoints, and free-cooling conditions to maximize savings while preserving the original intent of cooling or comfort.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule controls HVAC based on only one factor such as time or temperature, and additional occupancy and environmental signals are available.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply if added conditions make the automation unreliable, overly complex, or likely to violate comfort expectations.

patterns.pattern_description: Incorporate occupant thermal feedback, such as comfort buttons or app feedback, so energy-saving setpoint changes remain aligned with comfort preferences.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule modifies HVAC setpoints and user comfort feedback is available.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when feedback data is unavailable, unreliable, or would override critical safety or health constraints.

patterns.pattern_description: Replace rigid business-hour or clock-based HVAC operation with dynamic schedules that respond to real occupancy and environmental conditions.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a thermostat rule assumes occupancy based only on time of day or day of week.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when a fixed schedule is explicitly required or when sensor data is unavailable.

patterns.pattern_description: Use relaxed HVAC setpoints on weekends or holidays to save energy, but allow occupancy detection to restore comfort when people are present.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a rule maintains normal comfort conditioning during weekends, holidays, or other low-occupancy periods.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when the space is expected to be occupied, when occupancy cannot be detected, or when temperature must be maintained continuously.

patterns.pattern_description: Prefer occupancy-based transformations as broadly applicable green improvements because their savings are relatively consistent across different climates compared with adaptive comfort and night purge.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when choosing among possible green transformations and occupancy sensing is available.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when occupancy signals are unreliable or when the device must remain on regardless of occupancy.

patterns.pattern_description: Adjust the expected usefulness and aggressiveness of adaptive cooling based on climate and humidity, because dry hot climates benefit more while humid climates may require energy for moisture removal.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when outdoor climate or humidity data is available and a cooling setpoint rule can be adapted.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply when humidity control is essential, when outdoor conditions are outside the adaptive model range, or when the user requires a fixed setpoint.

patterns.pattern_description: Use night purge primarily in climates or situations with warm days and cool nights, marine climates, dry climates with large diurnal temperature swing, or buildings with useful thermal mass.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when nighttime outdoor air is cool enough and building characteristics allow stored cooling to reduce daytime HVAC demand.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply in climates or nights with insufficient cooling potential, excessive humidity, or conditions that would waste fan energy.

patterns.pattern_description: When using natural ventilation or night purge, include constraints so automated window opening does not compromise safety, security, or occupant comfort.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when generating rules that open windows or use natural ventilation for energy savings.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not open windows when security, weather, humidity, pollution, or user privacy constraints make opening unsafe or undesirable.

patterns.pattern_description: When a room or building becomes unoccupied, coordinate HVAC setback with smart plug shutdown for non-critical equipment to reduce both HVAC and plug-load electricity.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when both HVAC and plug loads are controlled in the same space and occupancy state is available.
patterns.when_not_to_apply: Do not apply to critical plug loads or when HVAC and plug-load actions could conflict with user intent.
